3A_Bibliographic.citation: Lin, S.-Y., Eaton, N. R., & Schleider, J. L. (2020). Unpacking associations between mood symptoms and screen time in preadolescents: A network analysis. Journal of Abnormal Child Psychology, 48, 1635-1647.

3B_Sample_Data.verbatim_sample_description: This study utilized the baseline data from the Adolescent Brain and Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study, Curated Annual Release 2.0 (N=11,875). The ABCD Study is designed to follow youths recruited at age 9 and 10 (M=9.91, SD=0.62) from 9987 families into their adolescence/young adulthood. The first wave was fielded in 2016-2018, where females composed 48% of the child sample. The baseline race/ethnicity composition was 49% non-Hispanic white, 17% non-Hispanic black, 23% Hispanic, 5% Asian, and 5% others.
3B_Sample_Data.notes: N=9986 from 9987 families (one child randomly sampled per family); actual N may vary slightly across analyses due to missing data

3C_Exposure.exposure_variable: Screen time by type: TV/movie, video, video game, texting, social media, video chatting, mature video game, R-rated movie
3C_Exposure.exposure_instrument: ABCD Screen Time Survey (combining two questionnaires on behavioral problems and school performance)
3C_Exposure.modalities: TV/movies, watching videos, playing video games, texting, visiting social media sites, video chatting, playing mature video games, watching R-rated movies
3C_Exposure.operationalization: Continuous (weighted mean of weekday x 5/7 and weekend x 2/7 for common types; 0-4 frequency scale for age-inappropriate types); treated as count (Poisson) or ordinal in network models
3C_Exposure.verbatim_exposure_description: The ABCD assessed screen time by combining content from two questionnaires that are associated with behavioral problems and poor school performance. Common types of screen time reported by children included: watching television or movies, watching videos, playing video games, visiting social media sites, texting, and video chatting. Screen time on a typical weekday and weekend day were measured separately. The scoring method was as follows: 0=no usage; 0.25=less than 30 min; 0.5=30 min; 1=1 h; 2=2 h; 3=3 h; and 4=more than 4 h per day. Average daily screen time was computed as the weighted mean of the screen time on a typical weekday (multiplied by 5/7) and weekend day (multiplied by 2/7).

3D_Outcome.primary_outcome: Mood symptoms (depressive mood, anhedonia, irritability, anger, elevated mood, decreased need for sleep) from K-SADS-5
3D_Outcome.outcome_instrument: Kiddie Schedule for Affective Disorders and Schizophrenia for DSM-5 (K-SADS-5) - computerized screening interview
3D_Outcome.base_rate_if_binary: Irritability: 3% (M=0.03); Depressive mood: 2% (M=0.02); Anhedonia: 5% (M=0.05); Elevated mood: 2% (M=0.02); Anger: 2% (M=0.02); Decreased need for sleep: 2% (M=0.02)
3D_Outcome.verbatim_outcome_description: Mood symptoms assessed in the K-SADS-5 screening interview included: depressive mood, anhedonia, and irritability for depression, and anger, decreased need for sleep, elevated mood and increased sexuality for mania. The computerized K-SADS-5 has shown promising psychometric properties and validity in assessing mood disorders. All symptoms were binary (0=absent, or 1=present).
3D_Outcome.notes: Increased sexuality was excluded because no children endorsed it

3E_Design_Analysis.model_type_detail: Mixed graphical models (MGM) via exponential families for network estimation; LASSO regularization; multiple linear regressions on bootstrapped networks for symptom connectivity/centrality analyses
3E_Design_Analysis.covariates_listed: BIS (behavioral inhibition), BAS-D (drive), BAS-FS (fun seeking), BAS-RR (reward responsiveness), parental monitoring
3E_Design_Analysis.verbatim_analysis_description: We applied l1-regularization (LASSO) and cross-validation to control for spurious relations in the MGM models. We used simulated random networks to test for significance. Sex differences in edges of mood symptoms and screen time were examined by permutation tests that compared edge weights of interest. We further conducted a series of network and regression analyses examining the association between screen time on mature video games and R-rated movies with the overall network connectivity and respective node strength.
3E_Design_Analysis.notes: Network analysis approach - effect sizes are edge weights from LASSO-regularized network models and regression coefficients from bootstrapped networks

3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: mature video games: b=0.02, t=9.25, p<0.001... with an R2 of 0.25 and 0.19, respectively
R-rated movies: b=0.05, t=17.34, p<0.001) with an R2 of 0.25 and 0.19, respectively

3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome: Decreased need for sleep node strength
Decreased need for sleep node strength

3G_Reporting_Quality.abstract_significance_verbatim: screen time involving age-inappropriate content was stably and significantly associated with various elevated mood symptoms
3G_Reporting_Quality.notes: Effect sizes are very small (b values in the range of 0.006-0.05) but this is not acknowledged. The paper uses network analysis which makes traditional effect size interpretation difficult. Uses mix of exact p-values and threshold language (p<.001).

3H_Recommendations.notes: Paper makes research recommendations (future studies should use longitudinal designs, EMA, symptom-level analyses) but does not issue clinical or policy recommendations.

3I_Rhetorical_Moves.discussion_characterization: The discussion emphasizes the nuanced, content-specific nature of screen time-mood associations. The key finding is that age-inappropriate content (mature video games, R-rated movies) showed stable associations with mood symptoms, while common screen time types did not. The paper explicitly acknowledges the cross-sectional limitation and does not claim causality. The authors note that different types of screen time have different relationships with different mood symptoms, advocating for a more granular approach to research. The discussion is research-oriented and does not overstate findings.
3I_Rhetorical_Moves.ambiguity_notes: Network analysis produces edge weights that are difficult to compare to traditional effect sizes. The regression coefficients (b) for the connectivity/centrality analyses are extremely small. Unclear whether the 'primary' analysis is the network estimation or the follow-up regression models.
